name: Kaveh
description: Kaveh, also known as Kaveh the Blacksmith (Kāveh Ahangar), is a pivotal figure in Persian mythology, representing the common people's revolt against tyranny and oppression. He is not strictly a deity in the same vein as Ahura Mazda or Mithra, but his legendary status elevates him to a quasi-divine position as a symbol of righteous rebellion and national liberation. Kaveh's story is deeply intertwined with the overthrow of the tyrannical king Zahhak, whose reign was marked by cruelty and the imposition of unjust laws. Kaveh, a simple blacksmith, bravely steps forward to challenge Zahhak after the king unjustly murders his son. His act of defiance sparks a widespread uprising, ultimately leading to Zahhak's downfall and the restoration of justice and order. Kaveh's leather apron, raised as a banner of revolt, becomes the Derafsh Kaviani, the national flag of Persia during various dynasties. He is a testament to the power of the common man and the importance of resistance against injustice. He embodies courage, resilience, and the unwavering pursuit of freedom.
subtitle: The Blacksmith of Justice

keyMyths.title: The Revolt Against Zahhak
keyMyths.description: Zahhak, a tyrannical king, ruled with an iron fist, demanding the brains of young men to feed the serpents growing from his shoulders. Kaveh, a blacksmith, loses his son to this cruel decree. Enraged and heartbroken, Kaveh publicly protests against Zahhak, using his blacksmith's apron as a banner. His act of defiance inspires others to join him, igniting a rebellion that sweeps across the land. This uprising ultimately leads to the capture of Zahhak and the restoration of the rightful king, Fereydun.

keyMyths.title: The Raising of the Derafsh Kaviani
keyMyths.description: After successfully leading the revolt against Zahhak, Kaveh's leather apron is transformed into a magnificent banner, the Derafsh Kaviani. This banner becomes a symbol of Persian national identity and is used by subsequent rulers as a standard in battle. The Derafsh Kaviani represents the triumph of good over evil, justice over tyranny, and the collective power of the people. It is a potent reminder of Kaveh's bravery and the importance of standing up against oppression.

keyMyths.title: Kaveh's Legacy
keyMyths.description: Although not a god in the traditional sense, Kaveh's influence extends far beyond his lifetime. He is remembered as a national hero, a symbol of resistance against tyranny, and a champion of the common people. His story is retold in countless poems, stories, and plays, inspiring generations of Persians to stand up for their rights and fight for justice. The Derafsh Kaviani, originating from his humble apron, continues to symbolize Persian identity and the enduring spirit of freedom.
keyMyths.source: Arthur George Warner and Edmond Warner, The Shahnama of Firdausi

sources.author: Ferdowsi
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Kaveh and the revolt against Zahhak.
